Erkannte Domänen: aviation

tasks.search_intent: Aktuellen Stand der Forschung zu Predictive Maintenance/PHM in der Luftfahrt inkl. Übersichtsarbeiten und Benchmarks finden.
tasks.task_rationale: Gibt den State-of-the-Art, Reifegrad und zentrale Methoden/Trends als Primärgrundlage der Beantwortung.

tasks.search_intent: Industrielle Implementierungen und Programme bei OEMs/Airlines/MRO (z.B. Engine Health Monitoring) mit Ergebnissen/ROI identifizieren.
tasks.task_rationale: Belegt den Entwicklungsstand durch reale Deployments, Nutzenkennzahlen und Skalierungsgrad in der Praxis.

tasks.search_intent: Relevante Standards und Leitfäden für aircraft condition monitoring, data exchange und PdM-Prozesse zusammentragen.
tasks.task_rationale: Erfasst normative Grundlagen/Datenaustauschformate, die den Reifegrad und die Umsetzbarkeit maßgeblich bestimmen.

tasks.search_intent: Regulatorische Anforderungen und Zulassungspraxis zu datengestützter Instandhaltung/MSG-3/CBM (EASA/FAA) recherchieren.
tasks.task_rationale: Klärt, was aktuell regulatorisch möglich/anerkannt ist und welche Nachweisführung für PdM in der Luftfahrt gilt.

tasks.search_intent: Patente zu PdM/PHM für Flugzeugstrukturen, Triebwerke und Avionik zur Trend- und Technologiereifeanalyse auswerten.
tasks.task_rationale: Ergänzt die Landscape um F&E-Trends, Akteure und Reifeindikatoren jenseits der veröffentlichten Literatur.

research_plan.reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den Reifegrad/State-of-the-Art von Predictive Maintenance (PdM) in der Luftfahrt. Dafür sind (a) technische Methoden und Sensorik/Health Monitoring, (b) datengetriebene Ansätze und Standards, (c) industrielle Implementierungen bei OEMs/Airlines/MROs sowie (d) regulatorische Rahmenbedingungen und Zulassungspraxis relevant. Der Suchplan priorisiert zuerst Übersichts- und Benchmark-Literatur, dann Standards/Regulierung, danach konkrete Programmen/Case Studies und schließlich Patente als Indikator für Technologietrends.

hypothesis_gate.reasoning: score=6/6; scope=2, comparative=0, temporal=1, evidence=2, ambiguity=1; llm_reasoning=Die Frage nach dem „aktuellen Entwicklungsstand“ ist breit (Methoden, Reifegrad, Anwendungen) und verlangt heterogene Evidenz aus Forschung, Industrie, Standards und Regulierung. Kein expliziter Vergleich zwischen Alternativen/Optionen, aber ein zeitlicher Blick auf jüngste Entwicklungen (v.a. seit ~2018/2019) ist nötig. Der Begriff „Entwicklungsstand“ ist moderat unscharf (Technikreife vs. Deployment vs. Zulassung), wird jedoch durch den Plan teilweise operationalisiert.

Support Data Sharing Develop, mature, and support technologies and organizations sup- porting data sharing, such as the ICDA. 8 Conclusions Everyone in this study recognized the potential for PdM and agreed that there was inadequate adoption. According to the Department of Energy, predictive maintenance program can reduce maintenance costs by as much as 30% (Ref. 6) or $3 Billion (Ref. 8). With input from SMEs and published sources, the authors identified five barriers preventing the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies. Targeted investments to address these barriers, such as those outlined in this document, will ultimately help break down these barriers and enable progress toward the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies and strategies in aviation. content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: requirements. Next, the team, which is composed of representatives from the aircraft manufacturer, airlines, and maintenance providers, review and evaluate the data in combination with a failure mode and effect analysis to develop maintenance tasks and intervals that are most cost-effective for the specific aircraft type. The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. One notable exception is jet engine maintenance. A significant amount of research has been put into PdM strategies for engines by General Electric (Ref. 15–17)[Abhinav Saxena, GE Aerospace Research, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Germany, 2023] and others. Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. This creates an economic incentive to

content.abstract_or_snippet: obilbranche zählen unter anderem die Bereiche Design- validierung, Vorhersage von Fahrzeugwartungszeiten, Optimie- rung der Kraftstoffeffizienz oder Fahrzeugleistungstests [29]. © Adobe Stock Grundlegende Aspekte des Digitalen Zwillings 2.3.3 Luftfahrtbranche Die Luftfahrtindustrie ist wie die Schifffahrt durch ein hohes Maß an technischer und betrieblicher Komplexität gekenn- zeichnet und umfasst ein vielschichtiges Ökosystem mit unterstützender Infrastruktur. Eingebettet in dieses System ist das sogenannte Digitale Flugzeug, die virtuelle Repräsentation eines Flugzeugs mit seinen geometrischen und physikalischen Eigenschaften unter Verwendung numerischer Simulations- modelle und -tools. Ziel ist es, die Konsistenz aller digitalen Daten und Modelle während des gesamten Lebenszyklus eines Flugzeugs zu erreichen – vom Entwurf über die Produktion und den Betrieb bis hin zur Stilllegung. Um dies zu erreichen, ist die Abkehr von Dateninseln, bei denen die Daten in verschiedenen Abteilungen und lokal bei externen Dienstleistern gespeichert sind, hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen und Datenformaten von elementarer Bedeutung [23]. Benchmarking, das Anomalien in den Betriebsparametern der Flugzeuge identifiziert. Diese bilden den Ausgangspunkt für die Einleitung von entsprechenden Korrekturmaßnahmen. Zu diesem Zweck betreibt beispielsweise der Flugzeughersteller Airbus eine eigene Plattform namens Skywise [34], die Daten von rund 9.500 Flugzeugen und mehr als 140 Fluggesellschaf- ten sammelt. Um einen möglichst umfassenden Überblick über das Produkt Flugzeug zu erhalten, sind neben den Fluggesell- schaften auch Zulieferer sowie mehr als 150 verschiedene Systeme an die Plattform angebunden. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass der Einsatz des Digitalen Zwillings in den betrachteten Branchen auf eine digitale Durch- gängigkeit aller im Produktlebenszyklus

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. The ATSaaS platform employs hybrid maintenance strategies, combining customiza- tion (for precise, airline-specific control of maintenance processes) and personalization (for scalable, AI-driven adaptive maintenance solutions). Large airlines with dedicated mainte- nance teams benefit from customizable service modules, while small and medium-sized airlines (SMAs) rely on personalized predictive analytics to optimize resource allocation and reduce maintenance overhead. This study explores the hybrid ATSaaS framework, which integrates AI-based fault detection, classification algorithms, and control methodologies to enhance main- tenance decision-making. The proposed ATSaaS platform provides a solution that enables stakeholders—including airlines, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) providers, and aviation regulators—to implement data-driven, scalable, and adaptive maintenance strategies that ensure higher fleet availability, lower operational costs, and improved safety standards. 1.2. Current Approaches to Data Exchange in Aviation Industry Aviation data management follows strict protocols and technical guidelines to main- tain dependability across all industry participants, from airlines to manufacturers and maintenance teams. These guidelines ensure systematic and protected methods for han- dling operational and service information. EASA (European Union Aviation Safety Agency) establishes regulatory frameworks relevant to aviation maintenance, including Part M (Continuing Airworthiness Require- ments), Part 145 (Maintenance Organization Approvals), and Part 21 (Certification of Aircraft and Components) [2]. These regulations mandate that maintenance data remain current, accessible, and subject to formal update procedures, ensuring compliance with safety and operational standards. The ATSaaS platform aligns with these requirements by integrating digital compliance tracking and real-time documentation updates, facilitating seamless adherence to regulatory obligations for both large airlines and smaller operators. Leading plane manufacturers have created sophisticated data management systems. For instance, Airbus Skywise categorizes information into some areas: technical aircraft data, such as flight reports and system monitoring outputs; service records including work orders and parts replacement documentation; operational flight data from recording systems; and technical documents including service updates and engineering records [3]. Boeing’s aircraft health management system (AHMS) features three components: live fault tracking, which monitors airborne communications addressing and reporting sys- Processes 2025, 13, 666 3 of 54 tem (ACARS) messages and sends real-time alerts; system performance tracking to assess aircraft operational efficiency; and configuration oversight for managing parts and modifica- tions [4
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems.pdf

content.abstract_or_snippet: Misra, RISE, 2023][Kai Wicke, DLR, 2023] 4. Support Data Sharing Develop, mature, and support technologies and organizations sup- porting data sharing, such as the ICDA. 8 Conclusions Everyone in this study recognized the potential for PdM and agreed that there was inadequate adoption. According to the Department of Energy, predictive maintenance program can reduce maintenance costs by as much as 30% (Ref. 6) or $3 Billion (Ref. 8). With input from SMEs and published sources, the authors identified five barriers preventing the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies. Targeted investments to address these barriers, such as those outlined in this document, will ultimately help break down these barriers and enable progress toward the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies and strategies in aviation. content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: requirements. Next, the team, which is composed of representatives from the aircraft manufacturer, airlines, and maintenance providers, review and evaluate the data in combination with a failure mode and effect analysis to develop maintenance tasks and intervals that are most cost-effective for the specific aircraft type. The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. One notable exception is jet engine maintenance. A significant amount of research has been put into PdM strategies for engines by General Electric (Ref. 15–17)[Abhinav Saxena, GE Aerospace Research, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Germany, 2023] and others. Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. This creates an economic incentive to

content.abstract_or_snippet: obilbranche zählen unter anderem die Bereiche Design- validierung, Vorhersage von Fahrzeugwartungszeiten, Optimie- rung der Kraftstoffeffizienz oder Fahrzeugleistungstests [29]. © Adobe Stock Grundlegende Aspekte des Digitalen Zwillings 2.3.3 Luftfahrtbranche Die Luftfahrtindustrie ist wie die Schifffahrt durch ein hohes Maß an technischer und betrieblicher Komplexität gekenn- zeichnet und umfasst ein vielschichtiges Ökosystem mit unterstützender Infrastruktur. Eingebettet in dieses System ist das sogenannte Digitale Flugzeug, die virtuelle Repräsentation eines Flugzeugs mit seinen geometrischen und physikalischen Eigenschaften unter Verwendung numerischer Simulations- modelle und -tools. Ziel ist es, die Konsistenz aller digitalen Daten und Modelle während des gesamten Lebenszyklus eines Flugzeugs zu erreichen – vom Entwurf über die Produktion und den Betrieb bis hin zur Stilllegung. Um dies zu erreichen, ist die Abkehr von Dateninseln, bei denen die Daten in verschiedenen Abteilungen und lokal bei externen Dienstleistern gespeichert sind, hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen und Datenformaten von elementarer Bedeutung [23]. Benchmarking, das Anomalien in den Betriebsparametern der Flugzeuge identifiziert. Diese bilden den Ausgangspunkt für die Einleitung von entsprechenden Korrekturmaßnahmen. Zu diesem Zweck betreibt beispielsweise der Flugzeughersteller Airbus eine eigene Plattform namens Skywise [34], die Daten von rund 9.500 Flugzeugen und mehr als 140 Fluggesellschaf- ten sammelt. Um einen möglichst umfassenden Überblick über das Produkt Flugzeug zu erhalten, sind neben den Fluggesell- schaften auch Zulieferer sowie mehr als 150 verschiedene Systeme an die Plattform angebunden. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass der Einsatz des Digitalen Zwillings in den betrachteten Branchen auf eine digitale Durch- gängigkeit aller im Produktlebenszyklus

The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. The ATSaaS platform employs hybrid maintenance strategies, combining customiza- tion (for precise, airline-specific control of maintenance processes) and personalization (for scalable, AI-driven adaptive maintenance solutions). Large airlines with dedicated mainte- nance teams benefit from customizable service modules, while small and medium-sized airlines (SMAs) rely on personalized predictive analytics to optimize resource allocation and reduce maintenance overhead. This study explores the hybrid ATSaaS framework, which integrates AI-based fault detection, classification algorithms, and control methodologies to enhance main- tenance decision-making. The proposed ATSaaS platform provides a solution that enables stakeholders—including airlines, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) providers, and aviation regulators—to implement data-driven, scalable, and adaptive maintenance strategies that ensure higher fleet availability, lower operational costs, and improved safety standards. 1.2. Current Approaches to Data Exchange in Aviation Industry Aviation data management follows strict protocols and technical guidelines to main- tain dependability across all industry participants, from airlines to manufacturers and maintenance teams. These guidelines ensure systematic and protected methods for han- dling operational and service information. EASA (European Union Aviation Safety Agency) establishes regulatory frameworks relevant to aviation maintenance, including Part M (Continuing Airworthiness Require- ments), Part 145 (Maintenance Organization Approvals), and Part 21 (Certification of Aircraft and Components) [2]. These regulations mandate that maintenance data remain current, accessible, and subject to formal update procedures, ensuring compliance with safety and operational standards. The ATSaaS platform aligns with these requirements by integrating digital compliance tracking and real-time documentation updates, facilitating seamless adherence to regulatory obligations for both large airlines and smaller operators. Leading plane manufacturers have created sophisticated data management systems. For instance, Airbus Skywise categorizes information into some areas: technical aircraft data, such as flight reports and system monitoring outputs; service records including work orders and parts replacement documentation; operational flight data from recording systems; and technical documents including service updates and engineering records [3]. Boeing’s aircraft health management system (AHMS) features three components: live fault tracking, which monitors airborne communications addressing and reporting sys- Processes 2025, 13, 666 3 of 54 tem (ACARS) messages and sends real-time alerts; system performance tracking to assess aircraft operational efficiency; and configuration oversight for managing parts and modifica- tions [4
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems.pdf

According to the Department of Energy, predictive maintenance program can reduce maintenance costs by as much as 30% (Ref. 6) or $3 Billion (Ref. 8). With input from SMEs and published sources, the authors identified five barriers preventing the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies. Targeted investments to address these barriers, such as those outlined in this document, will ultimately help break down these barriers and enable progress toward the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies and strategies in aviation. all_chunks.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

domain_applicability.reason: Abkürzungs-/Begriffsverzeichnis mit stark luftfahrtspezifischen Termen (MEL, MRB, HUMS, FAA); kaum methodischer, übertragbarer Inhalt.

Um dies zu erreichen, ist die Abkehr von Dateninseln, bei denen die Daten in verschiedenen Abteilungen und lokal bei externen Dienstleistern gespeichert sind, hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen und Datenformaten von elementarer Bedeutung [23]. Benchmarking, das Anomalien in den Betriebsparametern der Flugzeuge identifiziert. Diese bilden den Ausgangspunkt für die Einleitung von entsprechenden Korrekturmaßnahmen. Zu diesem Zweck betreibt beispielsweise der Flugzeughersteller Airbus eine eigene Plattform namens Skywise [34], die Daten von rund 9.500 Flugzeugen und mehr als 140 Fluggesellschaf- ten sammelt. Um einen möglichst umfassenden Überblick über das Produkt Flugzeug zu erhalten, sind neben den Fluggesell- schaften auch Zulieferer sowie mehr als 150 verschiedene Systeme an die Plattform angebunden. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass der Einsatz des Digitalen Zwillings in den betrachteten Branchen auf eine digitale Durch- gängigkeit aller im Produktlebenszyklus

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. The ATSaaS platform employs hybrid maintenance strategies, combining customiza- tion (for precise, airline-specific control of maintenance processes) and personalization (for scalable, AI-driven adaptive maintenance solutions). Large airlines with dedicated mainte- nance teams benefit from customizable service modules, while small and medium-sized airlines (SMAs) rely on personalized predictive analytics to optimize resource allocation and reduce maintenance overhead. This study explores the hybrid ATSaaS framework, which integrates AI-based fault detection, classification algorithms, and control methodologies to enhance main- tenance decision-making. The proposed ATSaaS platform provides a solution that enables stakeholders—including airlines, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) providers, and aviation regulators—to implement data-driven, scalable, and adaptive maintenance strategies that ensure higher fleet availability, lower operational costs, and improved safety standards. 1.2. Current Approaches to Data Exchange in Aviation Industry Aviation data management follows strict protocols and technical guidelines to main- tain dependability across all industry participants, from airlines to manufacturers and maintenance teams. These guidelines ensure systematic and protected methods for han- dling operational and service information. EASA (European Union Aviation Safety Agency) establishes regulatory frameworks relevant to aviation maintenance, including Part M (Continuing Airworthiness Require- ments), Part 145 (Maintenance Organization Approvals), and Part 21 (Certification of Aircraft and Components) [2]. These regulations mandate that maintenance data remain current, accessible, and subject to formal update procedures, ensuring compliance with safety and operational standards. The ATSaaS platform aligns with these requirements by integrating digital compliance tracking and real-time documentation updates, facilitating seamless adherence to regulatory obligations for both large airlines and smaller operators. Leading plane manufacturers have created sophisticated data management systems. For instance, Airbus Skywise categorizes information into some areas: technical aircraft data, such as flight reports and system monitoring outputs; service records including work orders and parts replacement documentation; operational flight data from recording systems; and technical documents including service updates and engineering records [3]. preview.query: Wie ist der aktuelle Entwicklungsstand von Predictive Maintenance bei Flugzeugen?
preview.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den Reifegrad/State-of-the-Art von Predictive Maintenance (PdM) in der Luftfahrt. Dafür sind (a) technische Methoden und Sensorik/Health Monitoring, (b) datenge…

input.query: Wie ist der aktuelle Entwicklungsstand von Predictive Maintenance bei Flugzeugen?
input.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den Reifegrad/State-of-the-Art von Predictive Maintenance (PdM) in der Luftfahrt. Dafür sind (a) technische Methoden und Sensorik/Health Monitoring, (b) datengetriebene Ansätze und Standards, (c) industrielle Implementierungen bei OEMs/Airlines/MROs sowie (d) regulatorische Rahmenbedingungen und Zulassungspraxis relevant. Der Suchplan priorisiert zuerst Übersichts- und Benchmark-Literatur, dann Standards/Regulierung, danach konkrete Programmen/Case Studies und schließlich Patente als Indikator für Technologietrends.

research_aspects.aspect: Technischer Stand von PHM/Sensorik & Condition Monitoring
research_aspects.description: Erfasst Reifegrad von Bord- und Boden-Sensorik, HUMS/ACMS/FOQA, Engine/airframe health monitoring sowie Diagnose/Prognose-Algorithmen als Kern der PdM-Fähigkeit.
Bewertet eingesetzte ML/Statistik-Ansätze, Datenqualität/Labeling, Digital Twins sowie Datenpipelines und Austauschformate/Architekturen, die PdM skalierbar machen.
Analysiert reale Programme (z.B. Engine Health Monitoring, AHM), Integrationsgrad in Wartungsprozesse, Nachweis von Nutzen (Dispatch Reliability, AOG-Reduktion, Kosten/ROI) und Lessons Learned.

research_aspects.aspect: Standards, Prozesse & Governance für zustandsbasierte Instandhaltung
research_aspects.description: Sammelt einschlägige Standards/Leitfäden (z.B. MSG-3/CM, Datenstandards) und ordnet sie in PdM-Prozessmodelle, Rollen und Verantwortlichkeiten ein.
Klärt Anforderungen an Continuing Airworthiness, Zulassung von CBM/PdM-gestützten Intervallen, Nachweisführung, Software/AI-Aspekte und Auditierbarkeit.

Support Data Sharing Develop, mature, and support technologies and organizations sup- porting data sharing, such as the ICDA. 8 Conclusions Everyone in this study recognized the potential for PdM and agreed that there was inadequate adoption. According to the Department of Energy, predictive maintenance program can reduce maintenance costs by as much as 30% (Ref. 6) or $3 Billion (Ref. 8). With input from SMEs and published sources, the authors identified five barriers preventing the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies. Targeted investments to address these barriers, such as those outlined in this document, will ultimately help break down these barriers and enable progress toward the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies and strategies in aviation. content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: requirements. Next, the team, which is composed of representatives from the aircraft manufacturer, airlines, and maintenance providers, review and evaluate the data in combination with a failure mode and effect analysis to develop maintenance tasks and intervals that are most cost-effective for the specific aircraft type. The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. One notable exception is jet engine maintenance. A significant amount of research has been put into PdM strategies for engines by General Electric (Ref. 15–17)[Abhinav Saxena, GE Aerospace Research, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Germany, 2023] and others. Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. This creates an economic incentive to

content.title: Studie Digitaler Zwilling Schiffbau Schiffsbetrieb 2023
content.abstract_or_snippet: obilbranche zählen unter anderem die Bereiche Design- validierung, Vorhersage von Fahrzeugwartungszeiten, Optimie- rung der Kraftstoffeffizienz oder Fahrzeugleistungstests [29]. © Adobe Stock Grundlegende Aspekte des Digitalen Zwillings 2.3.3 Luftfahrtbranche Die Luftfahrtindustrie ist wie die Schifffahrt durch ein hohes Maß an technischer und betrieblicher Komplexität gekenn- zeichnet und umfasst ein vielschichtiges Ökosystem mit unterstützender Infrastruktur. Eingebettet in dieses System ist das sogenannte Digitale Flugzeug, die virtuelle Repräsentation eines Flugzeugs mit seinen geometrischen und physikalischen Eigenschaften unter Verwendung numerischer Simulations- modelle und -tools. Ziel ist es, die Konsistenz aller digitalen Daten und Modelle während des gesamten Lebenszyklus eines Flugzeugs zu erreichen – vom Entwurf über die Produktion und den Betrieb bis hin zur Stilllegung. Um dies zu erreichen, ist die Abkehr von Dateninseln, bei denen die Daten in verschiedenen Abteilungen und lokal bei externen Dienstleistern gespeichert sind, hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen und Datenformaten von elementarer Bedeutung [23]. Benchmarking, das Anomalien in den Betriebsparametern der Flugzeuge identifiziert. Diese bilden den Ausgangspunkt für die Einleitung von entsprechenden Korrekturmaßnahmen. Zu diesem Zweck betreibt beispielsweise der Flugzeughersteller Airbus eine eigene Plattform namens Skywise [34], die Daten von rund 9.500 Flugzeugen und mehr als 140 Fluggesellschaf- ten sammelt. Um einen möglichst umfassenden Überblick über das Produkt Flugzeug zu erhalten, sind neben den Fluggesell- schaften auch Zulieferer sowie mehr als 150 verschiedene Systeme an die Plattform angebunden. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass der Einsatz des Digitalen Zwillings in den betrachteten Branchen auf eine digitale Durch- gängigkeit aller im Produktlebenszyklus

The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. The ATSaaS platform employs hybrid maintenance strategies, combining customiza- tion (for precise, airline-specific control of maintenance processes) and personalization (for scalable, AI-driven adaptive maintenance solutions). Large airlines with dedicated mainte- nance teams benefit from customizable service modules, while small and medium-sized airlines (SMAs) rely on personalized predictive analytics to optimize resource allocation and reduce maintenance overhead. This study explores the hybrid ATSaaS framework, which integrates AI-based fault detection, classification algorithms, and control methodologies to enhance main- tenance decision-making. The proposed ATSaaS platform provides a solution that enables stakeholders—including airlines, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) providers, and aviation regulators—to implement data-driven, scalable, and adaptive maintenance strategies that ensure higher fleet availability, lower operational costs, and improved safety standards. 1.2. Current Approaches to Data Exchange in Aviation Industry Aviation data management follows strict protocols and technical guidelines to main- tain dependability across all industry participants, from airlines to manufacturers and maintenance teams. These guidelines ensure systematic and protected methods for han- dling operational and service information. EASA (European Union Aviation Safety Agency) establishes regulatory frameworks relevant to aviation maintenance, including Part M (Continuing Airworthiness Require- ments), Part 145 (Maintenance Organization Approvals), and Part 21 (Certification of Aircraft and Components) [2]. These regulations mandate that maintenance data remain current, accessible, and subject to formal update procedures, ensuring compliance with safety and operational standards. The ATSaaS platform aligns with these requirements by integrating digital compliance tracking and real-time documentation updates, facilitating seamless adherence to regulatory obligations for both large airlines and smaller operators. Leading plane manufacturers have created sophisticated data management systems. For instance, Airbus Skywise categorizes information into some areas: technical aircraft data, such as flight reports and system monitoring outputs; service records including work orders and parts replacement documentation; operational flight data from recording systems; and technical documents including service updates and engineering records [3]. Boeing’s aircraft health management system (AHMS) features three components: live fault tracking, which monitors airborne communications addressing and reporting sys- Processes 2025, 13, 666 3 of 54 tem (ACARS) messages and sends real-time alerts; system performance tracking to assess aircraft operational efficiency; and configuration oversight for managing parts and modifica- tions [4
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems.pdf

Current Approaches to Data Exchange in Aviation Industry Aviation data management follows strict protocols and technical guidelines to main- tain dependability across all industry participants, from airlines to manufacturers and maintenance teams. These guidelines ensure systematic and protected methods for han- dling operational and service information. EASA (European Union Aviation Safety Agency) establishes regulatory frameworks relevant to aviation maintenance, including Part M (Continuing Airworthiness Require- ments), Part 145 (Maintenance Organization Approvals), and Part 21 (Certification of Aircraft and Components) [2]. These regulations mandate that maintenance data remain current, accessible, and subject to formal update procedures, ensuring compliance with safety and operational standards. The ATSaaS platform aligns with these requirements by integrating digital compliance tracking and real-time documentation updates, facilitating seamless adherence to regulatory obligations for both large airlines and smaller operators. Leading plane manufacturers have created sophisticated data management systems. For instance, Airbus Skywise categorizes information into some areas: technical aircraft data, such as flight reports and system monitoring outputs; service records including work orders and parts replacement documentation; operational flight data from recording systems; and technical documents including service updates and engineering records [3]. Boeing’s aircraft health management system (AHMS) features three components: live fault tracking, which monitors airborne communications addressing and reporting sys- Processes 2025, 13, 666 3 of 54 tem (ACARS) messages and sends real-time alerts; system performance tracking to assess aircraft operational efficiency; and configuration oversight for managing parts and modifica- tions [4

hypotheses.statement: In der zivilen Luftfahrt ist Predictive Maintenance (PdM) 2023 insgesamt noch nicht breitflächig umgesetzt; die dominanten Wartungsstrategien bleiben korrektiv und regelbasiert, ergänzt durch wenige Condition-Based-Maintenance-(CBM)-Anwendungen.
hypotheses.reasoning: NASA-TM20230000841 beschreibt den „Current State of Adoption“: trotz zahlreicher Initiativen gebe es „inadequate adoption“; Wartungsstrategien seien „primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications“.

hypotheses.statement: Der höchste Reifegrad von PdM in der Luftfahrt liegt derzeit im Triebwerksbereich (Jet Engines), während PdM für andere Flugzeugsysteme vergleichsweise weniger etabliert ist.
hypotheses.reasoning: Im NASA-Report wird Jet-Engine-Maintenance als „notable exception“ genannt, für die „a significant amount of research“ in PdM-Strategien (z.B. durch GE und andere) stattgefunden habe, im Gegensatz zu vielen anderen Systemen.

hypotheses.statement: Skalierbare PdM-Implementierung in der Luftfahrt hängt aktuell stark von zentralen Datenplattformen und der Überwindung von „Dateninseln“ ab; Airbus Skywise dient als Indikator für fortgeschrittene Datensammlung/-integration (u.a. tausende Flugzeuge, >140 Airlines, >150 Systeme).
hypotheses.reasoning: Die Digital-Zwilling-Studie betont für die Luftfahrt die Notwendigkeit standardisierter Schnittstellen/Datenformate zur Abkehr von Dateninseln und nennt Skywise als Plattform, die Daten von ca. 9.500 Flugzeugen sammelt und viele Teilnehmer/Systeme anbindet—ein Reifegrad-Indikator für Dateninfrastruktur als PdM-Enabler.

hypotheses.statement: Regulatorische Anforderungen an Continuing Airworthiness und Maintenance-Organisationen (EASA Part M/145/21) treiben den Bedarf an aktueller, zugänglicher, formal gepflegter Wartungsdokumentation und erschweren dadurch die schnelle, organisationsübergreifende Skalierung von AI-/PdM-Ansätzen ohne integriertes Compliance- und Daten-Governance-Design.
hypotheses.reasoning: Kabashkin beschreibt, dass EASA Part M/145/21 vorschreiben, dass Wartungsdaten aktuell, zugänglich und formalen Update-Prozessen unterliegen; der NASA-Report beschreibt die MRB/MRBR-Prozesse als industrie-regulatorisch kollaborativ und zentral für Wartungsprogramme. Zusammen stützt dies die Hypothese, dass PdM/AI nur mit eingebetteter Compliance/Datengovernance gut skalierbar ist.

Support Data Sharing Develop, mature, and support technologies and organizations sup- porting data sharing, such as the ICDA. 8 Conclusions Everyone in this study recognized the potential for PdM and agreed that there was inadequate adoption. According to the Department of Energy, predictive maintenance program can reduce maintenance costs by as much as 30% (Ref. 6) or $3 Billion (Ref. 8). With input from SMEs and published sources, the authors identified five barriers preventing the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies. Targeted investments to address these barriers, such as those outlined in this document, will ultimately help break down these barriers and enable progress toward the full-scale adoption of predictive maintenance technologies and strategies in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

content.abstract_or_snippet: requirements. Next, the team, which is composed of representatives from the aircraft manufacturer, airlines, and maintenance providers, review and evaluate the data in combination with a failure mode and effect analysis to develop maintenance tasks and intervals that are most cost-effective for the specific aircraft type. The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. One notable exception is jet engine maintenance. A significant amount of research has been put into PdM strategies for engines by General Electric (Ref. 15–17)[Abhinav Saxena, GE Aerospace Research, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Germany, 2023] and others. Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. Ziel ist es, die Konsistenz aller digitalen Daten und Modelle während des gesamten Lebenszyklus eines Flugzeugs zu erreichen – vom Entwurf über die Produktion und den Betrieb bis hin zur Stilllegung. Um dies zu erreichen, ist die Abkehr von Dateninseln, bei denen die Daten in verschiedenen Abteilungen und lokal bei externen Dienstleistern gespeichert sind, hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen und Datenformaten von elementarer Bedeutung [23]. Eingebettet in dieses System ist das sogenannte Digitale Flugzeug, die virtuelle Repräsentation eines Flugzeugs mit seinen geometrischen und physikalischen Eigenschaften unter Verwendung numerischer Simulations- modelle und -tools. Ziel ist es, die Konsistenz aller digitalen Daten und Modelle während des gesamten Lebenszyklus eines Flugzeugs zu erreichen – vom Entwurf über die Produktion und den Betrieb bis hin zur Stilllegung. Um dies zu erreichen, ist die Abkehr von Dateninseln, bei denen die Daten in verschiedenen Abteilungen und lokal bei externen Dienstleistern gespeichert sind, hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen und Datenformaten von elementarer Bedeutung [23]. Benchmarking, das Anomalien in den Betriebsparametern der Flugzeuge identifiziert. Diese bilden den Ausgangspunkt für die Einleitung von entsprechenden Korrekturmaßnahmen. Zu diesem Zweck betreibt beispielsweise der Flugzeughersteller Airbus eine eigene Plattform namens Skywise [34], die Daten von rund 9.500 Flugzeugen und mehr als 140 Fluggesellschaf- ten sammelt. Um einen möglichst umfassenden Überblick über das Produkt Flugzeug zu erhalten, sind neben den Fluggesell- schaften auch Zulieferer sowie mehr als 150 verschiedene Systeme an die Plattform angebunden. Abschließend ist zu konstatieren, dass der Einsatz des Digitalen Zwillings in den betrachteten Branchen auf eine digitale Durch- gängigkeit aller im Produktlebenszyklus
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/Studie Digitaler Zwilling Schiffbau Schiffsbetrieb 2023.pdf

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: requirements. Next, the team, which is composed of representatives from the aircraft manufacturer, airlines, and maintenance providers, review and evaluate the data in combination with a failure mode and effect analysis to develop maintenance tasks and intervals that are most cost-effective for the specific aircraft type. The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. One notable exception is jet engine maintenance. A significant amount of research has been put into PdM strategies for engines by General Electric (Ref. 15–17)[Abhinav Saxena, GE Aerospace Research, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Germany, 2023] and others. Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. This creates an economic incentive to
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript.pdf

content.title: Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems
content.abstract_or_snippet: smaller operators. The transition to AI-driven fault detection and classification provides an opportunity to enhance maintenance efficiency, reduce unnecessary interventions, and improve overall system reliability. The ATSaaS platform employs hybrid maintenance strategies, combining customiza- tion (for precise, airline-specific control of maintenance processes) and personalization (for scalable, AI-driven adaptive maintenance solutions). Large airlines with dedicated mainte- nance teams benefit from customizable service modules, while small and medium-sized airlines (SMAs) rely on personalized predictive analytics to optimize resource allocation and reduce maintenance overhead. This study explores the hybrid ATSaaS framework, which integrates AI-based fault detection, classification algorithms, and control methodologies to enhance main- tenance decision-making. The proposed ATSaaS platform provides a solution that enables stakeholders—including airlines, Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) providers, and aviation regulators—to implement data-driven, scalable, and adaptive maintenance strategies that ensure higher fleet availability, lower operational costs, and improved safety standards. 1.2. Current Approaches to Data Exchange in Aviation Industry Aviation data management follows strict protocols and technical guidelines to main- tain dependability across all industry participants, from airlines to manufacturers and maintenance teams. These guidelines ensure systematic and protected methods for han- dling operational and service information. EASA (European Union Aviation Safety Agency) establishes regulatory frameworks relevant to aviation maintenance, including Part M (Continuing Airworthiness Require- ments), Part 145 (Maintenance Organization Approvals), and Part 21 (Certification of Aircraft and Components) [2]. These regulations mandate that maintenance data remain current, accessible, and subject to formal update procedures, ensuring compliance with safety and operational standards. The ATSaaS platform aligns with these requirements by integrating digital compliance tracking and real-time documentation updates, facilitating seamless adherence to regulatory obligations for both large airlines and smaller operators. Leading plane manufacturers have created sophisticated data management systems. For instance, Airbus Skywise categorizes information into some areas: technical aircraft data, such as flight reports and system monitoring outputs; service records including work orders and parts replacement documentation; operational flight data from recording systems; and technical documents including service updates and engineering records [3]. Boeing’s aircraft health management system (AHMS) features three components: live fault tracking, which monitors airborne communications addressing and reporting sys- Processes 2025, 13, 666 3 of 54 tem (ACARS) messages and sends real-time alerts; system performance tracking to assess aircraft operational efficiency; and configuration oversight for managing parts and modifica- tions [4
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/Kabashkin - AI-Driven Fault Detection and Maintenance Optimization for Aviation Technical Support Systems.pdf

domain_applicability.reason: Wie hZVB…: generische AI-driven Maintenance-Optimierung/Personalisierung; wenig proprietäre Details, daher übertragbar.

updated_hypotheses.statement: In der zivilen Luftfahrt ist Predictive Maintenance (PdM) 2023 insgesamt noch nicht breitflächig umgesetzt; die dominanten Wartungsstrategien bleiben korrektiv und regelbasiert, ergänzt durch wenige Condition-Based-Maintenance-(CBM)-Anwendungen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Gute semantische Passung zur bereitgestellten Evidenz: NASA-TM20230000841 beschreibt den „Current State of Adoption“ als unzureichend („inadequate adoption“) und charakterisiert operative Wartungspolicies überwiegend als Kombination aus korrektiver und regelbasierter präventiver Wartung mit nur wenigen CBM-Anwendungen; damit deckt sich die Aussage, dass PdM 2023 nicht breitflächig umgesetzt ist und korrektiv/regelbasiert dominiert. Keine Gegenbelege in den Dokumenten; die Nennung vieler Plattforminitiativen wird im Report gerade als Teil von Bemühungen trotz geringer Skalierung dargestellt.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Der höchste Reifegrad von PdM in der Luftfahrt liegt derzeit im Triebwerksbereich (Jet Engines), während PdM für andere Flugzeugsysteme vergleichsweise weniger etabliert ist.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Teilweise gestützt, aber im bereitgestellten Evidenz-Ausschnitt nicht direkt belegt: NASA-TM nennt verschiedene PdM-Initiativen und (laut Hypothesenbegründung) Jet Engines als „notable exception“, jedoch liegt in den vorliegenden Snippets keine explizite Passage zum Reifegrad-Vergleich Triebwerk vs. andere Systeme vor. Daher derzeit nur gemischter Fit: plausibel im Kontext, aber Evidenzbasis hier zu schmal für „validated“.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Skalierbare PdM-Implementierung in der Luftfahrt hängt aktuell stark von zentralen Datenplattformen und der Überwindung von „Dateninseln“ ab; Airbus Skywise dient als Indikator für fortgeschrittene Datensammlung/-integration (u.a. tausende Flugzeuge, >140 Airlines, >150 Systeme).
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Hoher Fit: Die Digital-Zwilling-Studie betont für die Luftfahrt explizit die Notwendigkeit der Abkehr von „Dateninseln“ hin zu standardisierten Schnittstellen/Datenformaten als Voraussetzung für Lifecycle-Konsistenz. Sie nennt Airbus Skywise als Plattform, die Daten von ~9.500 Flugzeugen, >140 Airlines sammelt und >150 Systeme anbindet—das stützt sowohl die Rolle zentraler Datenplattformen als PdM/Anomalie-Benchmarking-Enabler als auch Skywise als Indikator fortgeschrittener Datenintegration. Keine widersprechenden Dokumente vorhanden.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Regulatorische Anforderungen an Continuing Airworthiness und Maintenance-Organisationen (EASA Part M/145/21) treiben den Bedarf an aktueller, zugänglicher, formal gepflegter Wartungsdokumentation und erschweren dadurch die schnelle, organisationsübergreifende Skalierung von AI-/PdM-Ansätzen ohne integriertes Compliance- und Daten-Governance-Design.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Moderater Fit: Kabashkin beschreibt strikte Protokolle/Guidelines im Aviation Data Management und verweist auf EASA-Regelwerke (Part M/145 u.a.) als Rahmenbedingungen; NASA-TM beschreibt den MRB/MRBR-Prozess als industrie- und regulatorisch-kollaborativ und als Basis für MEL/Wartungsprogramme. Daraus lässt sich ein Compliance-/Governance-Bedarf ableiten, jedoch belegen die Snippets nicht direkt, dass diese Anforderungen PdM/AI-Skalierung „erschweren“ oder ohne integriertes Compliance-Design nicht möglich ist. Daher aktuell eher plausibel als gesichert.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

challenge_findings.description: NASA-TM20230000841 konstatiert unzureichende PdM-Adoption und beschreibt Wartungspolicies überwiegend als korrektiv + regelbasiert, mit nur wenigen CBM-Anwendungen; das bestätigt die Hypothese zur fehlenden Breitenumsetzung (Stand 2023).

Die bereitgestellten Auszüge nennen PdM-Initiativen, enthalten aber keine explizite Evidenzstelle, die den höchsten PdM-Reifegrad im Triebwerksbereich gegenüber anderen Systemen belegt; derzeit zu schmale Textbasis für Validierung.

challenge_findings.description: Die Digital-Zwilling-Studie fordert explizit die Überwindung von Dateninseln durch standardisierte Schnittstellen/Formate und nennt Skywise mit Kennzahlen (~9.500 Flugzeuge, >140 Airlines, >150 Systeme) als Datenintegrationsplattform; das stützt die Abhängigkeit skalierbarer PdM von zentralen Datenplattformen.

Evidenz belegt regulatorische Rahmen (EASA Part M/145) und den MRB/MRBR-Prozess, aber nicht direkt die behauptete Bremswirkung auf organisationsübergreifende PdM/AI-Skalierung bzw. den konkreten Bedarf an integriertem Compliance-/Data-Governance-Design; Schlussfolgerung bleibt indirekt.

research_plan.reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf den Reifegrad/State-of-the-Art von Predictive Maintenance (PdM) in der Luftfahrt. Dafür sind (a) technische Methoden und Sensorik/Health Monitoring, (b) datengetriebene Ansätze und Standards, (c) industrielle Implementierungen bei OEMs/Airlines/MROs sowie (d) regulatorische Rahmenbedingungen und Zulassungspraxis relevant. Der Suchplan priorisiert zuerst Übersichts- und Benchmark-Literatur, dann Standards/Regulierung, danach konkrete Programmen/Case Studies und schließlich Patente als Indikator für Technologietrends.

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: requirements. Next, the team, which is composed of representatives from the aircraft manufacturer, airlines, and maintenance providers, review and evaluate the data in combination with a failure mode and effect analysis to develop maintenance tasks and intervals that are most cost-effective for the specific aircraft type. The MRB process is a collaborative effort between the industry and the regulatory authorities (e.g., FAA), with the goal of providing safe, reliable, and efficient maintenance programs. The MRB results in the development of a Maintenance Review Board Report (MRBR) which is then reviewed by the regulatory authorities and becomes the basis for the Minimum Equipment List (MEL) and the maintenance program of the aircraft type. The International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board (IMRBPB) is a group consisting of representatives from 10 global National Airworthiness Authorities (including FAA Flight Standards in the US) and the MPIG. They ensure that maintenance guidance is applied uniformly across the world, reconciling differences between nations airworthiness authorities. 1.3 Current State of Adoption PdM strategies have been investigated for years and have recently seen some success in non- aviation fields such as wind turbines or the oil and gas industry. Compared to aviation, these applications operate in a more repeatable, understood, and controlled environment. Several efforts are working to unlock the potential of PdM, including Airbus Skywise, Boeing Analytx, Collins Ascentia, Embraer Ikon, GE Predix, Honeywell Forge, KLM Prognos, Lufthansa AVIATAR, and Rolls-Royce Intelligent Engine. Furthermore, there has been limited success in the Helicopter Health and Usage Monitoring System (HUMS) (Ref. 13, 14) and military Condition Based Maintenance Plus (CBM+) communities (Ref. 11). Despite this, with a few exceptions, current aircraft maintenance strategies are primarily some combination of corrective and rule-based with a few CBM applications. One notable exception is jet engine maintenance. A significant amount of research has been put into PdM strategies for engines by General Electric (Ref. 15–17)[Abhinav Saxena, GE Aerospace Research, 2022][Kimon Abu-Taa, Rolls-Royce Germany, 2023] and others. Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. This creates an economic incentive to

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: . Unlike manufacturers of many other aircraft systems, the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) for engines typically is responsible for maintenance at a fixed price (per flight hour) [Ravi Rajamani, IDCA President]. This creates an economic incentive to develop and adopt PdM technologies to minimize maintenance costs. Some claim to be utilizing PdM. With a few exceptions, most of these PdM approaches are In some cases, there is a mislabeling of CBM rudimentary PdM approaches such as trending. techniques. Price Waterhouse Cooper found that “companies plans and ambitions regarding PdM have not yet resulted in a noticeable increase in its use among companies” (Ref. 9) In 2022, the FAA issued guidance on how to develop an Integrated Aircraft Health Management (AHM) System (Ref. 18). This follows earlier guidance from the IMRBPB (Ref. 19). This action has encouraged airlines to put more effort into developing and exploring the adoption of PdM and other AHM technologies (Ref. 20). Stakeholders in NASA, industry, and academia have been dissatisfied with the scale of adoption of PdM strategies. There is universal recognition of the advantages of PdM, but it true potential has yet to be unlocked. 2 Approach In order to better understand why PdM has not been adopted en masse, we collected and examined data from aviation industry experts on the barriers they faced in adopting predictive maintenance strategies. The goal was to understand the barriers preventing scale adoptions, and the most impactful work that academia, industry, and research organizations such as NASA could do to address those barriers. Data collection was conducted via a literature survey and discussions with 24 Subject Matter Experts (SME)’s from academia, industry, and government, as well as ad-hoc discussions at the PHM Society and other industry conferences. In an attempt to attain comprehensive data, we tried to cover the majority of stakeholders: • Operators (e.g., Airlines) • Regulatory Organizations (e.g., FAA) • Standard Bodies (e.g., SAE, IEEE) • Aircraft Manufacturers (e.g., Boeing) • OEMs (e.g., GE) • Independent Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul (MRO) Organizations (e.g., Rolls-Royce Germany) • Professional Societies (e.g., PHM Society, IEEE) • Research Institutions (e.g., Universities) Note that in this document input from these discussions are referenced using the following [
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript.pdf
